EXFOR No. = 31405.003. Journal article = Bao et al., 50 Years Nucl.Fission Conf., Washington D.C., 1989, Vol.2, p.951 (1989).The data by Bao et al. are in EXFOR sub-entries 31405.003. The data were published as part of proceedings~\cite{Bao1989} after Mannhart’s evaluation. Mannhart rejected those data because as is stated in Ref.~\cite{Mannhart2008presentation}: "The data of Bao et al.. are still quoted as preliminary and indicate difficulties in the proper correction of the lithium resonance." Otherwise, the data are faily well described.Bao et al. used a 0.95-cm thick (45 mm diameter) $^6$Li glass detector (NE912) to count neutrons. The neutron-detector efficiency is expilicitly given in Table 1 of Ref.~\cite{Bao1989}; it was simulated via Monte Carlo codes and compared to Lajtai's efficiency curve~\cite{Lajtai1990} who used the same detector type and dimensions. More recent MCNP simulations~\cite{NeudeckerTaddeucci2019} verified the efficiencies for most energies except for 200 keV (the $^6$Li peak). There Lajtai's efficiency is more than 10\% higher. While both simulations have limitations (missing environmental effects), it is interesting to note that Lajtai data show an increase at these energies that is not observed by Poenitz. Multiple scattering and other backgrounds were estimated by measuring with and without a Cu shadow cone. The TOF path was 30 cm long and was determined with an uncertainty of 1 mm. Fission fragments were detected in an 99.4\% efficient mini-ionization chamber. 252Cf was on don a 0.05-mm thick Pt backing with 0.1 mm diameter; it had an activity of 3.8e+3 f/s. The time resolution was 1.8 ns.
Corrections were undertaken for many major effects except for the shadow-bar background and finalized detector response.Detector efficient and energy uncertainties (relative to the PFNS) along with statistical uncertainties were provided in EXFOR. 1\% background uncertainties for gammas were mentioned, 0.2\% and 0.15\% are assumed for random coincidences and multiple scattering based on the correction size.One major concern is how the Cu shadow bar was used. It was situated between the fission amd neutron detector without shielding. That would cause a lot of scattering that was not corrected for the background measurement. Also, Lajtai et al. and Bao et al. assumed the shadow cone to be a perfect absorber for neutrons emitted from the fission chamber. Reference~\cite{NeudeckerTaddeucci2019} showed that this is not the case and estmated the combined effect om the PFNS to be 10\% which we take as additional uncertainty. Another issue is that the detector response was only simulated including the material of the crystal and not the surrounding.  One concern is the pronounced Li-6 peak that affects results until 500 keV. We rejected Boldeman Li-6 data because of similar behavior.The data set is adopted for the ML analysis but not for the new standards evaluation as they are preliminary Dyachenko.
Author: Bao
Tang
Fan
Cao
attributes.ConcernAIACHNE_text: The data are listed as preliminary because their detector efficiency is not final (not fully independently validated). One concern is the pronounced Li-6 peak that affects results until 500 keV. We rejected Boldeman Li-6 data because of similar behavior. One major concern is how the brass shadow bar was used. It was situated between the fission amd neutron detector without shielding. That would cause a lot of scattering that was not corrected for the background measurement. Also, Lajtai et al. assumed the shadow cone to be a perfect absorber for neutrons emitted from the fission chamber. Reference~\cite{NeudeckerTaddeucci2019} showed that this is not the case and estimated the combined effect on the PFNS to be 10\% which we take as additional uncertainty. Multiple scattweing is only approximatelty calculated for the crystal but not the surrounding. Another issue is that the detector response was only simulated including the material of the crystal and not the surrounding. Also, Fig.~2 of~\cite{Lajtai1990} shows a smearing of the detector response to higher energies; only a  smearing to lower energies is expected. Mistakes in the response function could especially affect PFNS values above the $^6$Li resonance.

EXFOR No. = 14477.003. Journal article = E. Blain et al., Physical Review, Part C, Nuclear Physics, Vol.95, p.064615 (2017).\ 
                The data were published after Mannhart’s evaluation as a journal article. The key new element                 of the Blain et al. is that they used a new method to determine the fission signal (delayed stop                 signal) of the TOF measurement. They used the multiple gamma-tagging method: If a coincidence of                 two out of four BaF2 gamma-detectors were fired by prompt fission gamma rays from the sample                 within a time window of 3 ns, this was seen as detecting fission. The influence of delayed                 gammas was reduced by requiring coincidence, while alpha-particle background can be eliminated                 by this method. The experimental team also fabricated a fission chamber to counter-checking this                 new method and got a very similar 252}Cf PFNS lending trust to the new method. The efficiency of                 this new fission detection method is distinctly lower (36.8%) than compared to the fission chamber                 (80%). They used as a neutron detector an EJ-204 scintillator for measurements from 50 keV--2 MeV                 (TOF path length of 48 cm, 0.5 inch thick and 5-inch diameter), while an EJ-301 scintillator was                 used for 0.5--7 MeV (TOF path length of 50 cm, 3-inch thick and 5-inch diameter). The detector                 efficiency of the EJ-301 scintillator was measured via the LINAC and the 235U fission cross section                 and then validated within 2% via simulations using SCINFUL. The neutron-detection efficiency of                 EJ-204 was only simulated via SCINFUL. The neutron detectors were located at 45 degree relative to                 the perpendicular plane of the 252Cf deposit. The point-sized 252Cf deposit (18.4 ng) was mounted in                 the middle of the BaF2 detectors and had an activity of 9000 f/s. The time resolution of the                 detection system was 3 ns FWHM. Corrections were made for most effects except for deadtime (likely                 small) and the 2-dimensional detector response; the latter is a larger effect. Uncertainties                 provided in EXFOR encompass detector efficiency (2% from the comparison of simulation and                 experiment) and background uncertainties. We have reached out to the PhD advisor of Blain and have                 not yet heard back on additional partial uncertainties. One concern is that the detector                 efficiency was measured in another surrounding than their PFNS measurement; i.e., the detector                 efficiency does not factor in the effect of multiple scattering and attenuation of material                 between the sample and the scintillators. This effect could be larger given the close distances                 of the particular set-up. Another issue is that the gamma-tagging method relies on MCNP-Polimi                 simulations of the coincidence which in turn depends on poorly known gamma-fission coincidences.                 Another concern is the relatively large gamma-ray background (Fig. 9 of the Reference) which                 renders the provided uncertainties questionably low. We accept the measurement for now awaiting                 additional partial uncertainties. We will increase background uncertainties.
Author: Blain
Daskalakis
Block
Danon
ConcernAIACHNE_text: fission detection efficiency depends on MCNP-Polimi simulations with poor gamma-fission correlation data
background rather large, unc. too small
detector efficiency was measured in other surrounding

attributes.documentation: EXFOR No. = 40418.007. Journal article = Conf. on Nucl. Data for Sci. and Technol.                 ,Antwerp 1982, p.465 (1982). The data by Blinov are here are described in                 Refs.~\cite{Blinov1973,Blinov1977,Blinov1982,Blinov1977b,Blinov1984,Batenkov1983}.                 There are many sub-entries in EXFOR for the same measurement series. We identified                 sub-entry 40418.007 as the one that Mannhart used by comparing the                 measurement description and data points. We assume that data in sub-entry                 40418.002 are superseeded by 40535 entries. 40418.003 and 40418.004 are                 marked as preliminary, 40418.005 and 40418.006 are digitized from                 conference proceedings with lilltle uncertainty information. 40418.007                 offers detailed uncertainty information. 40418.008 is a combination of                 measurement at three flight path lengths (25,50, and 100 cm) which renders                 the analysis of time resolution inaccurate. We follow Mannhart's example                 and use 40418.007. This decision was taken based on the data being final                 and offering detailed uncertainties. Neutrons were measured using a four                 $^{235}$U fission chambers measured at three distances (0.25, 0.50, and                 1.0~m, measured for 2, 4, and 10~days, respectively) from the $^{252}$Cf                 fission chamber.  A second stage of measurements were also performed                 outdoors and 9~m above floor scattering sources for distances of 0.25~m                 for 2~days, and 0.50~m for 21~days, but with one $^{235}$U neutron detector.                Fission chamber efficiency was determined using a Manganese bath along with                 Si detectors measurements with 1o angular resolution.  Measurements of the                 angular fission fragment detection efficiency were made as well, to assess                 the effect of choosing specific neutron detector angles. Corrections were                 made for all effects listed above; for each highly-detailed discussions are                 provided. Dats set 40418.007 was measured wit a 50-cm flightpath (2.5 mm                 uncertainty) and a total time resolution of 1.32 ns. Additionally,                 uncertainties were provided for counting statistics, the $^{235}$U(n,f)                 cross sections, multiple scattering corrections (air and structural                 material of detectors), background, radom-coincidence background, for the                 anisotropy correction of the fission fragments and corrections of kinematic                 effects. Deadtime and impurity uncertainties are taken from templates. The                 data are provided relative to a 1.42~MeV Maxwellian, the results of this                 work show a ratio near unity from 0.050-7.0~MeV, with a declining slope to                 a ratio near 0.80 at 10~MeV.  The results are smooth, and do not appear to                 display features suggestive of a misunderstanding of the detector response.                 There are no major concerns.  Furthermore, this is the only measurement to                 use $^{235}$U fission chambers as neutron detectors, thereby decoupling                 these data from other measurements typically using plastic scintillators,                 liquid scintillators, or Li-glass detectors. These data were accepted, and                 will be an important component for obtaining a new evaluation of the                 $^{252}$Cf PFNS.
Author: Blinov
Vitenko
Tuz
Kazarinov
Krisyuk

attributes.documentation: EXFOR No. = 40535.002. Journal article = Blinov et al. INDC(CCP)-195,                 1, (1983). The data by Blinov from EXFOR entry 40535 are                 described in Ref.~\cite{Blinov1980}. There are four sub-entries, each                 corresponds to a different flight-path length and energy range: 002 at                 6.25 cm (0.3--60 keV), 003 at 12.5 cm (10--201.1 keV), 004 at 25 cm                 (63.9 keV--1.006 MeV) and 005 at 50.0 cm (171 keV-–1.974 MeV). Everything                 else stays the same leading to four highly correlated data sets. None of                 them were used by Mannhart who used data from sub-entry 40418.007. We assume                 that data in sub-entry 40418.002 are superseeded by 40535 entries. We are                 unsure why Mannhart never used the latter data. He did not cite to them. So,                 maybe he was unaware of the data? We think they are reliable and take them.                 Neutrons were measured using a $^{6}$Li(Eu) crystal as neutron detector and a                 gas scintillation counter to detect fission fragments with two layer samples of                 $^{252}$Cf over a measurement time of 110 days. The joint timme resolution was                 given with 1.5 ns. The neutron-detection response was calculated via MC                 simulations using ENDF/B-V.0 $^6$Li(n,alpha) cross sections. These                 measurements were made for very low $E_\mathrm{out}$, where usually backgrounds                 and multiple scattering are major corrections. These effects were minimized by                 using no shielding and shadow bar and the specrometer electronic circuit.                 Multiple scattering on air, walls and the detector were MC simulated and measured                 (using a second detector with doubled mass), and relative corrections were supplied                 for the 6.25-cm measurement. The assumed Mawellian was 1.42 MeV. Corrections                 were made for nearly all effects listed above; it is unknown if the angular                 distribution of fission fragments was accounted for when calculating the                 fission-detector efficiency. Partial uncertainties for correction were not                 povided. I assume 20\% of the correction. The gamma background was supposed to                 be no more of 0.5%, the random-coincidence background was 0.07\% at 100 keV and                 6\% at 5 keV. Mutiple-scattering corrections were 0.1\% at 100 keV  7% at 10 keV,                 and 2\% at 1 keV. The contribution of impurities was determined to be so minimal                 that I assume 0\% uncertainty. The total uncertainties in EXFOR contain statistical                 and correction uncertainties---not accounting for the ENDF/B-V.0 monitor                 uncertainties that define the messing detector response uncertainties. Given that                 and assumptions above, statistical uncertainties can backed out and ENDF/B-V.0                 monitor uncertainties are added. Energy uncertainties are explicitly given.                 These data were accepted, and will be an important component for obtaining a                 new evaluation of the $^{252}$Cf PFNS.
Author: Blinov
Vitenko
Yurevich

Sample.Contaminants: 0.001 fissions from Curium and other Cf isotopes (most from Cf-250)

EXFOR No. = none. Journal article = Nuclear Science and Engineering,Vol.106, p. 377 (1990), USA. The data by Boettger et al. are not yet in the EXFOR database.So, we work with data and total uncertainties read from Mannhart's proceeding~\cite{Mannhart1984}. Further information is taken from Refs.~\cite{Boettger1987,Boettger1990,Boettger1983}.
- Neutrons from $^{252}$Cf(sf) were detected with four large NE213 scintillators, 5.08~cm thick $	imes$ 25.4~cm diameter.  Detectors were placed at 12.5 degree increments relative to the normal of the fission fragment detector, and at a distance of 12.00(2)~m. Each neutron detector was placed behind massive neutron shielding at the exist of collimation holes.  Neutron detectors themselves were all within a single room, separated by approximately 2.66~m from neighboring detectors, and with an exit collimation hole behind the detector.  Neutron efficincies were assessed using the NEFF and NRESP codes, with monoenergetic response comparisons to measurements from proton-recoil telescope measurements.  The $^{252}$Cf source for measurements carried out from 1981-1982 \cite{Boettger_1983} and from 1985--1986 \cite{Boettger_1990} are both parallel-plate ionization chambers, but have different construction and activity.  Differences of these chambers are described in Table~I of Ref.~\cite{Boettger_1990}. The effect on the PFNS from changing angles for neutron detection was studied, and no individual correction was required for the neutron detectors. Results following both sets of measurements are shown separately for each valid detector in Ref.~\cite{Boettger_1990}. Data from all detectors and from both measurements all appear to agree well within uncertainties. Mannhart statistically combined all measurement runs from 1981-1982 and 1985--1986 by adequately weighting each with their statistical and systematic uncertainties. Relative to a 1.42~MeV Maxwellian, the data are near unity in the 2-6~MeV range, with a decline to a ratio of approximately 0.75-0.85 at 14~MeV.  This trend is consistent with most other measurements in this range, as well as the Mannhart evaluation itself. Only total unceertainties could be read from Fig.~2 of ~\cite{Mannhart1984}, but extensive calculations and studies for sources of systematic errors and uncertainties were carried out for analysis of these data. Partial uncertainties are described~\cite{Boettger1987,Boettger1990,Boettger1983} for the neutron and fission detector efficiences, attenuation, multiple scattering, background and counting statistics along with TOF resolution anf TOF length uncertainties. Impurity and deadtime uncertainties were not provided, and were assumed from templates, but are small. The background uncertainties were only described approximately (2\% at lowest $E_\mathrm$ and then increasing), but could be backed out from total uncertainties. This measurement was exceptionally thorough; it provided an extensive list of uncertainties and potential sources of error.  The primary concerns concern the potential for scattering between neutron detectors behind the heavy shielding. In some cases, the nearest detectors are approximately 1~m away from each other. This close proximity could introduce multiple scattering between neutron detectors---especially considering the large volume of the scintillators used.  In cases, where neutron detector are 2.66 m apart, multiple scattering between them is likely a second-order effect compared with neutron scattering from walls and floors.  As data are provided at $E_\mathrm{out} >$ 2 MeV, these issues likely generate only a modest error in the results. The data set is accepted for the new standards evaluation.
Author: Bottger
Klein
Chalupka
Strohmaier

EfficiencyDeterminationMethod: confirmed from 3-12 MeV with n-p scattering data within 3%

attributes.DeadtimeDeterminationMethod: Delayed FF detector signal to be stop signal

attributes.documentation: EXFOR No. = 30775.003. Journal article = Boldeman et al., Nuclear Science and Engineering, Vol.93, p.181 (1986). The data by Boldeman~\emph{et al.} \cite{Boldeman_1986} appear in EXFOR sub-entries 30775.003 ($^6$Li, $E_\mathrm{out} <$  3 MeV) and 30775.002 (liquid scintillator, $E_\mathrm{out} >$ 1 MeV. Mannhart mean values agreeing with EXFOR mean values, except for 6 data points around the $^6$Li peak, the highest two data points of sub-entry 30775.003 and 3 unspecified data points of 30775.002. This work summarized eight experiments to measure the $^{252}$Cf PFNS: seven measurements using a 5.08~cm diameter times 2.54~cm thick NE-102 plastic scintillator with lower energy limits ranging from 1.0-2.0~MeV and upper limits from 13.7--14.7~MeV, and one measurement using a 5.08~cm diameter $	imes$ 2.0~mm thick Li-glass detector over 0.124--2.660~MeV energy limits.  NE-102 and Li-glass measurements were made with neutron flight paths of 0.40 and 3.015~m, respectively.  Fission events were measured with a methane-filled parallel-plate avalanche counter (PPAC).  The PPAC efficiency for fragment detection was measured with a Marion counter.  No collimation was used between the PPAC and the neutron detector in order to avoid scattering off of the collimator itself.  Measurements were made with a raised, 5 m high experiment area in order to avoid neutron scattering from the floor, with a 90 mm shadow bar used for NE-102 measurements only. Corrections for detector dead time, air attenuation, delayed neutrons and $\gamma$ rays, air-scattered neutrons, timing resolution, and fission fragment loss below threshold were all included in the analysis, with a thorough discussion.  The seven NE-102 measurements are combined to form a single result, and are reported combined with the Li-glass results.  The results are noted to compare reasonably well with a 1.42~MeV Maxwellian, but show notable and energy-dependent shape deviations from this trend.  The broad peak in the Li-glass data about a centroid of approximately 240~keV with a sharp drop from 300--400~keV and a slow rise to near unity, relative to a 1.42~MeV Maxwellian, are perhaps the most notable features.  Data from 1.0--5.0~MeV show a roughly flat ratio slightly above unity within statistics relative to the same Maxwellian, followed by a slow decline to a ratio of approximately 0.9 at 10~MeV.  These features of the NE-102 data agree with other measurements in the same ranges.  Data above 10~MeV vary between Maxwellian ratios of 0.754 and 1.072, but with large uncertainties. Unfortunately, no detailed partial uncertainties are provided. Statistical and total uncertaintites are identical to the digit given in the EXFOR entry, which is unrealistic. I assume 10% of the deadtime correction as unc., and other unc. (background, multiple scattering, and detector efficiency unc., etc.) using templates. The NE-102 scintillator efficiency was measured with the associated particle method at a Van de Graaff accelerator.  There was no mention of keeping the experimental environment the same as in the PFNS measurement, though at high outgoing energies this is likely not an issue.  The definition of ``high outgoing energies'' depends on the experimental environment involved.  On the other hand, the Li-glass efficiency measurement, also measured at a Van de Graaff accelerator, was explicitly stated to be as close to identical to the PFNS measurement as possible.  This statement was likely a reflection of the increased effect from neutron scattering expected for lower outgoing neutron energies.  The Li-glass data also show suspicious features resembling the $^6$Li($n$,$t$) resonance near 240~keV and a sharp decline above this resonance.  The NE-102 data also show some structure in the 1--5~MeV range, but this is only within 1--2\%.  Lastly, a 1.07~MeV Maxwellian was ``folded''. Similar to Mannhart, we reject Li-glass data points of Boldeman with structures related to the $^6$Li($n$,$t$) reaction itself.  The NE-102 data were accepted for this evaluation.
Author: Boldeman
Clancy
Culley
ConcernAIACHNE_text: The response was approximated by folding a Maxwellian.
Structures comensurate wit the 6Li peak are seem in data.

attributes.documentation:
EXFOR No. = 40874.003. Journal article =
The data from the group encompassing Starostov, Botsov and Nefedov~\cite{Starostov1983, Botsov1983, Nefedov1983, Starostov1985} are stored in several EXFOR entries following the neutron detector used. Mannhart rejected the data but it is unclear why.
Data were taken in two cycles. For cycle 1, a miniaturized ionization chamber was used (98\% efficiency), while cycle 2 used a gas scintillation detector (97\% efficiency) to detect fission fragments. For cycle 1, an anthracene detector (height of 0.4 cm and diameter of 1.8 cm, TOF-path length: 51 cm) was used from 0.1--2 MeV, a stilbene scintillator (height of 3 cm and diameter of 7 cm, TOF-path length: 231.3 cm) from 1.4--8 MeV and a plastic scintillator (height of 12 cm and diameter of 20 cm, TOF-path length: 611 cm) from 3--12 MeV. They are stored in EXFOR sub-entries 40874.002, 40871.005 and 40872.002, respectively which had to be identified by their energy range. Data in 40874.003 and 40874.004 were measured in the second cycle preliminary with an ionization chamber from 0.01--5 MeV (height of 0.8 cm and diameter of 10 cm, TOF-path length: 12.4--40 cm, time resolution: 4.8 ns) and agas scintillation ionization chamber (height of 0.01 cm and diameter of 11 cm, TOF-path length: 10.4--29.5 cm, time resolution: 2.5 ns). The time resolution was 4.2 ns for cyle-1 measurements. The assumed Mawellian was 1.417 MeV. The neutron-detector response was simulated with $^1$H and $^{12}$C data for the anthracene detector. $^{252}$Cf data was used for the stilbene and plastic scintillator which is a circular argument, while $^{235}$U(n,) cross secitions were used to determine the neutron-detector efficiency of cycle-2 measurements.
Corrections were described for many effects listed above; it is unknown if deadtime, sample decay, transmission through the shadow cone for background determination, and alpha background were accounted for in corrections. The anthracene and stilbene data agree with Mannhart evaluated data so well, and thus with the bulk of accepted experimental data that major corrections seem to have been correctly applied. Plastic data disagree noticeably.
Partial uncertainties are provided for most major effects, counting statistics, background, multiple scattering, detector efficiency, and time resolution. TOF length uncertainties are also given and it is stated that corrections for angular distribution (and, hence, we assume the related uncertainties) are negigible. We take deadtime and impurity uncertainties from templates.
The data measured with the anthracene detector and in the second cycle (above 0.15 MeV) were accepted for obtaining a new evaluation of the $^{252}$Cf PFNS and ML. Stilbene and plastic data cannot be taken into account for both due to the circular assumption to define the detector efficiency.
Author: Boytsov
Starostov
attributes.ConcernAIACHNE_text: They used U-235(n,f) cs as monitor reaction for obtaining the detector efficiency that has structures below 150 keV. That is the energy range we cut it down to.
attributes.AverageChannelWidthDetermination: measurement (determination of the shape of time distribution in transmission measurements)

attributes.documentation:
EXFOR No. = 22202.003+22202.004+22202.005. Journal article = Chalupka et al., Nuclear Science and Engineering, Vol.106, Issue.3, p.367 (1990).The data by Chalupka et al. are in EXFOR sub-entries 22202.003 (9--12.76 MeV), 22202.004 (13.98--27.10 MeV), 22202.005 (9--15.52 MeV)
, 22202.006 (9--18.94 MeV), and 22202.007 (9.99--20.02 MeV). The different EXFOR entries correspond to different energy ranges 
or bias settings (2.0, and 2.75 MeV). They can be combined considering correlations if not the same or overlapping energy ranges 
are used; otherwise, one would double count data as they stem from the same measurement. The journal publications were published 
after Mannhart’s evaluation, but he considered these data along with those of Maerten et al. for inclusion.The main aim of this measurement was to counter-check the upturn of the PFNS above 20 MeV seen by the Maerten et al. 
group which was contrary to the evaluation by Mannhart. They identify as main potential issue in that measurement above 20 MeV 
background from cosmic radiation. In order to reduce this specific background, Chalupka et al. performed their measurement 
in a mine in Bad Bleiberg, Austria which is 1000 m below a mountain known as a rich harvesting grounds for lead and tin. 
In essence, they had a 1000-m thick natural Pb shield to reduce random-coincidence background. One 25.4x5.08-cm scintillation
detector of type NE213 was used for all runs to count neutrons. The efficiency of this detector was low (20%). The efficiency
values were calculated by the MC technique using NEFF-4 up to 20 MeV. For 18-29 MeV, they used the 1H cross section 
data adjusted to NEFF results between 16-18 MeV, which agree well with today's JENDL-5.0 data. The light output curves 
were calibrated with 22Na, 60Co, and 208Tl Compton edge energies The TOF path was 279.6 cm long and had uncertainty of 2 mm.
The neutron detector was located at 0 degree relative to the perpendicular plane of the 252Cf deposit. Fission fragments were
detected in an  99.5\% efficient fission chamber. The 252Cf deposit was a layer of 15 mm diameter on platinum backing with 15 mm
diameter; it had an activity of 1.5643x10e5 f/s. The time resolution of the detection system was 1.0 ns FWHM. It could be that 
the detectors used were those used for the measurement of Boettger et al.Corrections were made for all known effects, and detailed partial uncertainties were provided for all uncertainties either
explicitly or stating that they were 0.There were some minor concerns: One was that the carbon cross section used in NEFF-4 agreed with JENDL-5.0 only to 7% (total)
and 9% (inleastic), however, the influence should be small as the main cross section used if H. Also, it was not completely clear
in how far the 252Cf PFNS spectrum was used for the detector response. The authors of this data set could, unfortunately, no clarify
this anymore. As these are minor concerns, and many important corrections were performed and detailed uncertainties are provided, 
we accept the data until 25 MeV, which is the limit of our evaluation.
Author: A.Chalupka
L.Malek
S.Tagesen
R.Boettger
attributes.ConcernAIACHNE_text: We are not sure how Maxwellian of T=1.42 was used to get the detector response. There could be a circular argument there. There could be scattering from the shadow cone leading to neutrons from water and iron. Gammas will have been taken care of by PSD cuts.
attributes.DeadtimeDeterminationMethod: Delayed FF detector signal to be stop signal

attributes.documentation:
EXFOR No. = 20401.003. Journal article = Atomkernenergie, Vol.22, p.84 (1973). 
There are a two data sub-entries in EXFOR for the same measurement series; each corresponds to a different fission detector. Otherwise, they are nearly the same leading to two highly correlated data sets. Data set 20401.003 corresponds to using a plastic scintillator for measuring fission gammas, while 20401.004 uses scintillator foils for measuring fission fragments. None of them were used by Mannhart. It is not documented why but the data set is not as detailed described as many of the accepted data sets. Also, there are possible biases in the data.
Neutrons were measured using a liquid scintillator (NE224) as neutron detector. The $^{252}$Cf sample was canned and had an activity of 3e+5 f/sec. The joint timme resolution was given with 1.5 ns (FWHM) with a TOF length of 162 cm. The neutron-detection effiency was measured relative to the T(p,n), D(d,n), 9Be(alpha,n), H(n,el), 7Li(p,n), and T(d,n) reactions in a van-de-Graaf nrutron beam. 
No corrections for the resolution nor multiple scattering were undertaken. Terry Taddeucci calculated these effects and found them to become substantial below 300 keV and around 8--10 MeV respectively. We add template uncertainties for those. Gamma background was not corrected for delayed gammas which should be small, but the simplified corrections for other gamma background might affect the data at late times.
The data deviate below 300 keV where Taddeucci et al. have shown that multiple scattering in the shielding plays a role via MC simulation. There are structures from 2-4 MeV. In this energy range, Knitter interpolates the detector efficiency with a smooth function, but the 12C(n,el) cross section has structures there and will appear in the plastic. Also, at high Eout, we expect problems in gammas.
The total uncertainties in EXFOR contain statistical and detector efficiency (2-5\%) uncertainties. Given that and assumptions above, statistical uncertainties can backed out. Energy uncertainties are explicitly given. Many uncertainties are added from templates with substantial ones estimated for background and multiple scattering.
The data are accepted for the ML analysis but rejected for the standards evaluation given their multiple issues.
Author: Knitter
Paulsen
Liskien
Islam
ConcernAIACHNE_text: Below 300 keV, where Terry has shown that multiple scattering in the shielding plays a role via MC simulation. 2-4 MeV is the energy range where he interpolates the detector efficiency with a smooth function, but the 12C(n,el) cross section has structures there and will appear in the plastic. Only scarce details are provided about the angular measurement that is tricky to do. Also, at high Eout, we expect problems in gammas.

attributes.documentation: EXFOR No. = 114431.002. Journal article = N. Kornilov, INDC(USA)-108,2015.                 The data were published after Mannhart’s evaluation as an INDC report.                Kornilov uses one scintillation detector of type NE213 for most runs to                 count neutrons, except for run 1 where three detectors of this type were                 used. The neutron detector(s) had a radius of 6.35 cm and height of 5.08 cm.                The efficiency of this detector was low with 15–30\%. The efficiency values                 of both neutron detectors were calculated by the MC technique using GEANT-4                 and NEFF-7 leveraging the dynamic threshold method to go up to 20 MeV with                 high accuracy. The light output of the detector was measured with a white                 neutron source. The TOF path was 400 cm long and had uncertainty of 5 mm.                 From a schematic drawing, it looks like in runs with one neutron detector,                 it was located at 0 degree relative to the perpendicular plane of the 252Cf                 deposit. Fission fragments were detected in an 99\% efficient ionization                 chamber. The 252Cf deposit was a layer of 10 mm diameter on a stainless                 steel electrode with 25 mm diameter; it had an activity of 2.2–3.94e4 f/s.                 The variation in activity is caused by the fact that the measurement took                 4500 hours of data over a period of 4 years in several runs. Due to the long                 measurement time, Kornilov accounted for the decay of the sample and measured                 also the detector’s stability. The time resolution of the detection system was                 not provided. Corrections were made for gamma-ray background,                 neutron-multiple scattering and attenuation in the sample and surrounding,                 decay of the sample, neutron-detection efficiency, fission detector                 efficiency (including anisotropy of fragment emission and absorption in                 the sample) and deadtime. Uncertainties provided in EXFOR are not specified,                but private communication with the author indicates that they are statistical                in nature. The author also mentioned that the deviation of experimental data                 obtained via the dynamic threshold method results from evaluated 252Cf(sf)                 PFNS by Mannhart could be seen as the methods systematic uncertainty                 (3\% below 3 MeV, 2.0\% for 2–7 MeV, 2.5\% from 7–10 MeV, and 4.0\% for 10–13 MeV).                I ignore his "method uncertianties" as they were obtained by comparison to                 Mannhart's evaluation which is a circular argument for us. Instead, I                 use the template uncertainties. One major concern for a standard evaluation                 is that values for many uncertainty sources are not provided, including the                 time resolution which is a major uncertainty source at high energies. Another                 issue is that the simulation to account for multiple scattering and the                 detector response is incomplete as only a simplified model of the detector                 was used for the MC simulation. This could lead to biases in the experimental                 data starting from a few MeV. Also, no correction for alpha background was                 mentioned, and very little on corrections for random coincidences. One can                 assume that PSD (performed for reducing gamma-ray background) would likely                 cut alpha background and the contribution should small. Random coincidences                 could bias the result above 12 MeV. In this energy range, the largest                 deviations can be seen in Fig. 24 of the INDC report. Also, it is unclear                 from Fig. 3 in the literature whether all gamma rays were correctly subtracted                 which could again bias the resulting data above 12 MeV. Given the concerns                 raised above on potential biases and the missing uncertainties, we decided                 to take the data of Kornilov below 12 MeV as validation data set for the                 evaluation rather than as direct input.
Author: N.V.Kornilov
attributes.ConcernAIACHNE_text: alpha (small), random-coincidence background (issue above 12 MeV), gammas not fully corrected (above 12 MeV), detector efficiency not completely simulated as too simplified set-up (everywhere, especially medium-range of the experiment)
MissingCorrections: alpha background, random-coincidence background, gammas background
attributes.DeadtimeDeterminationMethod: Delayed FF detector signal to be stop signal

attributes.documentation: EXFOR No. = 41158.002. Journal article = Lajtai et al., Nucl. Instrum.                 Methods in Physics Res., Sect.A, Vol.293, p.555 (1990). The data by Lajtai                 et al. are in EXFOR sub-entries 41158.003 (data as ratio to Maxwellian)                 and 41158.002 (shape data, neutron-detector efficiencies and their                 energy-dependent uncertainties). The data were published as a journal                 article~\cite{Lajtai1990} after Mannhart’s evaluation. What Mannhart terms                 ``Lajtai data'' in Ref.~\cite{Mannhart1986} corresponds to preceeding                 Dyachenko data of EXFOR entries 40875002 and 40875.003 by the same group.                 Lajtai data superseed Dyachenko data~\cite{Otuka:privcommObninsk}. Lajtai                 et al. used a 0.95-cm thick (45 mm diameter) $^6$Li glass detector (NE912)                 to count neutrons. The neutron-detector efficiency is expilicitly given in                 Table 1 of Ref.~\cite{Lajtai1990}; it was simulated via Monte Carlo codes                 for a 0.0835-cm thin $^6$Li glass detector (NE908). The efficiency for the                 NE912 detector was derived by a relative measurement to the NE908                 measurement. The NE912 detector was not directly simulated to avoid                 limitations in accurately computing multiple scattering and attenuation.                 More recent MCNP simulations~\cite{NeudeckerTaddeucci2019} verified the                 efficiencies for most energies except for 200 keV 200 keV (the $^6$Li peak).                 There Lajtai's efficiency is more than 10\% higher. While both simulations                 have limitations (missing environmental effects), it is interesting to note                 that Lajtai data show an increase at these energies that is not observed by                 Poenitz. A NE913 $^7$Li detector (only sensitive to $\gamma$ rays), of the                 same size as of the NE912 detector, measured the $\gamma$ background also                 registered by the latter. All other backgrounds were obtained by measuring                 with and without a brass shadow cone. The TOF path was 30 cm long and was                 determined with an uncertainty of 1 mm. Fission fragments were detected in                 an 99\% efficient fission chamber. The 252Cf deposit was a thin film of 7 mm                 diameter on a 0.05-mm thick Pt backing with 10 mm diameter; it had an                 activity of 1e+4 f/s. The time resolution was 4 ns. Corrections were                 undertaken for many major effects except for the anisotropy of fission                 fragment emission, the shadow-bar background and neutron attenuation and                 multiple scattering in the sample. Total uncertainties provided in EXFOR                 contain statistical uncertainties (foreground and background),                 uncertainties in the detector-response calculation (2\%), in the                 neutron-detection efficiency (given explicitly), and the fission-detection                 efficiency (3\%), TOF length (very small) and the solid angle (2\%). Only                 deadtime uncertainties need to be assumed from templates. One major concern                 is how the brass shadow bar was used. It was situated between the fission and                 neutron detector without shielding. That would cause a lot of scattering that                 was not corrected for the background measurement. Also, Lajtai et al. assumed                 the shadow cone to be a perfect absorber for neutrons emitted from the fission                 chamber. Reference~\cite{NeudeckerTaddeucci2019} showed that this is not the                 case and estmated the combined effect om the PFNS to be 10\% which we take as                 additional uncertainty. Another issue is that the detector response was only                 simulated including the material of the crystal and not the surrounding. Also,                 Fig.~2 of~\cite{Lajtai1990} shows a smearing of the detector response to higher                 energies; only a  smearing to lower energies is expected. Mistakes in the                 response function could especially affect PFNS values above the $^6$Li resonance.                 The data set is adopted for the new standards evaluation replacing preliminary                 Dyachenko data.

EXFOR No. = 30969.002. Journal article = Nuclear Science and Engineering,Vol.106, p.353 (1990), USA. The data by Maerten et al. are in EXFOR sub-entry 30969.002 (measured at 0 degree) and 30969.003 (measured at 60 degree)were published as a jornal publication~\cite{Maerten1990} contrary to EXFOR sub-entries 31854.002 and 31854.003 by the same group that were previously published as laboratory reports and proceeding~\cite{Maerten1984, Maerten1983, Maerten1982a, Maerten1982b, Maerten1982c}. The 30969.002 data were finalized after Mannhart's evaluation, but were recommended by him to be taken into account. This is the 0 degree measurement, which is strongly correlated with the 60 degree measurement. Maerten et al. recorded neutron spectra as a function of TOF and sctillator pulse height; PSD corrected for part of the background (from alphas, $\gamma$s, and random coincidences). The team used two neutron detectors of type NE213 at two different angles and TOF paths: (a) A 12.7-cm thick and 12.7-cm diameter scintillator was used at 0 degree with a TOF path length of 5.905 m and a time resolution of 1.5 ns. (b) A 3.81-cm thick and 12.7-cm diameter scintillator was used at 60 degree with a TOF path length of 3.275 m  and a time resolution of 1.3 ns. The neutron-detector efficiency (undocumented) is simulated via a Monte Carlo by Stanton~\cite{Stanton:1971} using light output curves by~\cite{Cecil:1979}. The authors carefully adjusted the simulation to consider the detector signal resolution (light output in terms of proton recoil equivalent). Also, they absolutely calibrated neutron detection efficiencies of both detectors against their response on 14-MeV neutrons from a DT neutron generator. The neutron source strength has been determined by counting the associated alpha-particles produced in the DT reaction (solid state detector positioned at a fixed backward angle)~\cite{Maerten:2023privcom}.   The detector response accounts for multiple scattering and attenuation in air, the surrounding, and detector walls. Fission fragments were detected as stop signal (delayed by 200 ns) in an 99.2$\pm$0.2\% efficient minitiuarized fission chamber. The $^{252}$Cf deposit had an activity of 70000 f/s. The fitted Maxwellian to these data had a temperature of 1.374 MeV.
Corrections were applied for nearly all effects mentioned above; it is only unknown if the decay of the sample was corrected for. Additionally,the following corrections are listed in the journal article: gamma peak correction (negligible), TOF channel dependent background due to non-correlated stop signals (negligible), unfolding correction due to time resolution, bin-width correction, and fragment detection efficiency. Statistical and systematic uncertainties are provided in the EXFOR entry 30969.002 based on Table~II of Ref.~\cite{Maerten1990}. The systematic uncertainties contain uncertainties of the neutron detection efficiency, energy scale, energy bin width, light-output calibration and all corrections. Reference~\cite{Maerten1990} cites to \cite{Maerten1984} for some partial uncertainties. Hence, we take from 31854.002 partial uncertainties for bin width correction, energy scale,  unfolding correction due to time resolution, multiple-scattering correction, light-output bias, and neutron detector efficiency, and back out unaccounted for systematic uncertainties. Impurity uncertainties were not provided, and were assumed from templates, but are small. One major concern of entry 31854.002 (previous data~\cite{Maerten1984}) was that the neutron-detecor efficiency was derived in a circular fashion by using the NBS $^{252}$Cf spectrum as input for validating the detector response. This was corrected here~\cite{Maerten1990} by undertaking measurements of the detector response wit a DT generator that was also measured with the associated particle technique~\cite{Maerten:2023privcom}. Given that and the thoughtful consideration of all necessary corrections, we accept data set 30969.002~\cite{Maerten1990} for the new standard evaluation and reject previous data~\cite{Maerten1984}. We also reject data above 20 MeV from~\cite{Maerten1990}; the data rely on extrapolating the detector response beyond the DT measurement and on simulations using carbon cross sections that changed significantly above 15 MeV since the mid-eighties. Not surprisingly, the data above 20 MeV show an unexpected upturn.
Author: Maerten
Richter
Seeliger
Fromm
Boettger
Klein

attributes.documentation: EXFOR No. = 14278.002. Journal article = Conf. on Nucl. Data for Sci. and Technol.                 ,Antwerp 1982, p.465 (1982). The data by Poenitz and Tamura are in EXFOR sub-entries                 14278.002 and 14278.003 that should be the same but differ because of the quality of                 reading them from file. They should be the same data as read from Figs.~3--4 of                 Mannhart's conference proceeding. The main paper is a conference proceeding and the data were                 marked as preliminary; no follow-up measurement was reported nor found in Poenitz’s boxes.                Poenitz and Tamura 	extit{et al.} used two different black neutron detectors---scintillators                 of type NE213.  The smaller one (7.62 cm radius and 17.78 cm height) was used from                 0.2-4 MeV with a flight-path of 258 cm, while the larger one (10 cm radius and 37 cm                 height) was used for 0.7-10 MeV with a flight path of 347 cm. Both were of high efficiency,                with 83-98\% for the smaller one and 77-96% and were located at 0 degree relative to the                 perpendicular plane of the $^{252}$Cf deposit. The efficiency values of both neutron detectors                 were calculated by the MC technique and experimentally verified with the associated-particle                 technique. Fission fragments were detected in an  71% efficient gas-scintillation counter                 with an activity of 1.43$	imes$10$^5$ f/s, containing a 252Cf deposit on a 0.0254 cm                 thick Ta backing. The total time resolution of the combined detection system was high with                 4~ns (FWHM). The fitted Maxwellian to these data had a temperature of 1.439 MeV.                 It seems that most of the required corrections were accounted for. Uncertainties                 provided in EXFOR are total only---likely because the data were considered to be                 preliminary. However, the journal article offers more information about                 uncertainties and corrections; the following effects were estimated to be                 negligible: $^{252}$Cf migrating in the chamber (4.0 $	imes$ 10$^{-3}$ \%),                transmission from the shadow-bar ($<$ 0.1\%), neutrons inscattering from the                 main collimator ($<$ 0.1\%), neutrons inscattering from the first shield                 collimator ($<$ 0.2\%), neutron scattering in the the sample, delayed $\gamma$                 and alpha background. The measured and simulated neutron-detector response                 agreed with $<$ 1.2\% for 0.5--0.9 MeV and 0.7\% at 14.1 MeV, which one could                 take as a crude estimate for associated uncertainties. The TOF length                 uncertainty was 0.5--1 mm with a fitted Maxwellian of 1.439 MeV. A part of                 the background uncertainties can be gleaned from information on the                 fission-detector efficiency, namely, that 20$\pm$ 2\% losses in efficiency                 are due to alpha pile-up and 9$\pm$ 3\% due to total fragment absorption                 loss on fission detector efficiency. Concerns were raised about the amount                 of shielding, and the shadow bar in the measurement. But the corrections                 were well-done, and the assembly was well-contained (closed environment),                 and the corrections should not pose an issue. There might be a small-angle                 effect of the collimator but it is very unlikely for that to happen. No                 other issue was noted for this measurement except the few explicit                 uncertainties and that it was called preliminary.
Author: Poenitz
Tamura
